questions.question: Which age group is at peak risk for unintentional poisoning?
options: A. Neonates
B. Toddlers (1-3 years)
C. School-age children
D. Adolescents
questions.rationale: Toddlers are at peak risk due to their mobility, curiosity, and tendency to mouth objects, combined with accessible household substances.

questions.question: What should field assessment of a suspected ingestion prioritize?
options: A. Waiting for definitive lab identification before any assessment
B. Identifying the substance, amount, and time of exposure, and bringing containers to the hospital
C. Ignoring the exposure if the child appears well
D. Inducing vomiting in all cases
questions.rationale: Field assessment should focus on identifying substance, amount, and timing when possible, and bringing physical evidence (containers/pills) to the hospital.

questions.question: A child has altered mental status, pinpoint pupils, and respiratory depression. What toxidrome does this suggest?
options: A. Anticholinergic
B. Opioid
C. Sympathomimetic
D. Cholinergic
questions.rationale: Pinpoint pupils with respiratory depression and altered mental status is the classic opioid toxidrome.

questions.question: Why is button battery ingestion especially dangerous?
options: A. It only causes minor, self-limited irritation
B. A lodged battery can cause severe tissue injury within hours via an electrical current effect
C. Button batteries pass through the GI tract without any risk
D. It is dangerous only if swallowed with food
questions.rationale: A lodged esophageal button battery can cause severe injury within hours through an electrical current effect, not just direct chemical burn.

questions.question: How should a suspected button battery ingestion be managed if the child currently appears asymptomatic?
options: A. No transport needed since symptoms are absent
B. Still treated as a time-critical emergency requiring rapid transport and hospital notification
C. Wait 24 hours to reassess
D. Manage at home with observation only
questions.rationale: Button battery ingestion is treated as time-critical regardless of current symptoms, since injury can progress even in an asymptomatic child.

questions.question: Why are children more vulnerable to heat illness than adults?
options: A. Higher surface-area-to-mass ratio, less efficient sweating, dependence on caregivers to escape heat
B. Children sweat more efficiently than adults
C. Children have a lower surface-area-to-mass ratio
D. Heat illness does not occur in children
questions.rationale: A higher surface-area-to-mass ratio, less efficient sweating, and reliance on caregivers to remove them from heat increase pediatric heat illness risk.

questions.question: Why is a child left in a hot vehicle a particularly dangerous scenario?
options: A. Vehicle interior temperatures rise slowly, giving ample time to intervene
B. Internal vehicle temperatures can become life-threatening within minutes
C. This scenario poses no unique risk compared to being outdoors
D. Only adults are at risk in hot vehicles
questions.rationale: Vehicle interiors can reach life-threatening temperatures within minutes, making this a high-stakes, preventable pediatric emergency.

questions.question: Why are infants especially vulnerable to hypothermia?
options: A. Higher surface-area-to-mass ratio plus limited ability to shiver for heat generation
B. Infants generate excess body heat compared to adults
C. Infants have thicker subcutaneous fat than adults
D. Hypothermia does not affect infants
questions.rationale: Infants share the pediatric surface-area-to-mass vulnerability and have a limited ability to shiver, increasing hypothermia risk.

questions.question: Which age groups are at highest risk for drowning?
options: A. Toddlers (bathtubs, pools) and adolescents (open water, risk-taking/alcohol)
B. Only neonates
C. Only school-age children
D. Drowning risk is equal across all pediatric ages and contexts
questions.rationale: Toddlers (bathtubs, pools) and adolescents (open water, often with risk-taking or alcohol) represent the highest-risk drowning groups.

questions.question: What is the core physiologic problem in drowning?
options: A. Hypothermia
B. Hypoxia
C. Hypoglycemia
D. Hypertension
questions.rationale: Drowning is fundamentally a respiratory event, with hypoxia as the primary problem to treat.

questions.question: When should spinal motion restriction be applied after a submersion event?
options: A. In every submersion case regardless of mechanism
B. Only if the mechanism suggests trauma, such as a diving injury
C. Never, regardless of mechanism
D. Only in adolescents
questions.rationale: Spinal motion restriction after submersion is applied selectively, based on a mechanism suggesting trauma (e.g., diving into shallow water).

questions.question: Should a well-appearing child be transported after a submersion event?
options: A. No, if the child appears completely normal
B. Yes — pulmonary complications can evolve over hours even in a well-appearing child
C. Only if the child requests transport
D. Only if hypothermia is present
questions.rationale: Delayed pulmonary complications can develop after submersion even in children who appear well initially, so transport for observation is recommended.

questions.question: What is the general field management framework across toxic and environmental emergencies?
options: A. Identify the exposure/mechanism, support ABCs, remove from ongoing hazard, bring materials to hospital, transport promptly
B. Treat only the presenting symptom without further assessment
C. Delay transport until the child is fully asymptomatic
D. Rely solely on caregiver reassurance to determine need for transport
questions.rationale: This bundle of priorities — exposure/mechanism identification, ABC support, hazard removal, bringing evidence, and prompt transport — applies across toxic and environmental emergencies.

questions.question: Why does PEPP recommend a lower threshold for transport/observation in pediatric toxic/environmental emergencies compared to adults?
options: A. Children are more likely to fabricate symptoms
B. Children can appear deceptively well before deteriorating
C. Pediatric transport is always free of cost considerations
D. There is no difference in threshold between children and adults
questions.rationale: Children's capacity to appear well before rapid deterioration (e.g., button battery, submersion, early heat illness) supports a lower threshold for transport/observation.
